_comment: Tab 5 (CROSS-CONNECTOR) chart specs for the Benchmark v2 Kafka dashboard, DWH Superset (432).
Consumed by the Stage-B build (script or browser operator). Layout follows
docs/benchmark-v2-plan.md §8 Tab 5 sketch + docs/benchmark-v2-contract.md §6:
a permanent CAVEAT BANNER (markdown); the rows/s 2-series trend; the server-cost
ch_insert_cpu_seconds_per_Mrows 2-series trend; parts_per_insert 2-series;
merge_amplification 2-series; the EFFICIENCY TABLE (latest-30d medians, metric|Spark|
Kafka|ratio); and an UNMATCHED CONTEXT table so the tab is informative while the
matched side is empty. EVERY chart carries `description` (what it shows / how to read /
direction of goodness) per the #42 standard.
SERIES: the two connector lines are keyed by connector_label ('Spark' | 'Kafka Connect').
DATASETS: v_xconn (tall per-run cross-connector view) + v_xconn_efficiency (30d-median
ratio table built ON v_xconn). ADDITIVE-ONLY: all new assets; Tab 5 reads verdict/alert
truth from NOWHERE (it is the descriptive payoff comparison) — the per-connector Tab 1s
stay the single source of verdict/alert truth.
MATCHED-DATASET DEFAULT (contract §5/§6): the comparison charts + efficiency table
DEFAULT-FILTER matched_dataset = 1. TODAY that side is HONESTLY EMPTY (Kafka runs 10M,
Spark ~100M — no shared (dataset,rows_expected) bucket). This is CORRECT and ACCEPTED:
structure-first; a Kafka 100M graduation is a deliberate future instrument event that
will populate the matched side automatically (the rule self-maintains). The empty-state
markdown note + the UNMATCHED context table keep the tab honest and useful meanwhile.
ENVIRONMENT-CLASS CAVEAT (contract §6): every chart surfaces environment_class beside
clickhouse_version; the classes differ today (Spark=production, Kafka=staging), so a
permanent banner warns that absolute cross-connector numbers straddle a prod/staging
boundary. H/P ratio GATES on each connector's own Tab 1 are unaffected.

datasets.v_xconn: tall per-run cross-connector view (per run × canonical metric): value, metric_name, connector/connector_label, arm/tier, dataset, rows_expected, comp_bucket, bucket_connectors, matched_dataset, environment_class, clickhouse_version, target_region, compute_region, flagged, flag_reason, pair_ts, pair_seq — BOTH connectors, arm=head, tier=1, §2.2 headlines aliased to rows_per_sec
datasets.v_xconn_efficiency: latest-30d MEDIAN per (metric, connector) + ratio (built ON v_xconn, matched-only): metric_name, unit, spark_median, kafka_median, ratio_spark_over_kafka, spark/kafka_environment_class, spark/kafka_clickhouse_version, spark_n, kafka_n — feeds the efficiency table; EMPTY today (no matched bucket)

tab_filters._comment: Tab-wide native filters. matched_dataset defaults to 1 on the COMPARISON charts + efficiency table (only comparable rows). The UNMATCHED context table pins matched_dataset=0. connector_label is the series key, not a filter.

matched_dataset_default.name: matched_dataset
matched_dataset_default.default: 1 (matched only — the comparable cross-connector rows). The comparison charts hard-scope matched_dataset=1 in their `filters`; the UNMATCHED context table hard-scopes matched_dataset=0. v_xconn_efficiency is matched-only by construction (no filter needed).
matched_dataset_default.note: TODAY matched_dataset=1 selects ZERO rows (Kafka 10M vs Spark 100M). The comparison charts render empty by design; the empty-state markdown + context table explain why.

charts.markdown:
## Tab 5 — Cross-Connector: Spark vs Kafka Connect (the payoff)

**Read this first.** This tab compares the two connectors on the *shared* perf schema — same metric definitions, same integrity rules, same server-cost accounting.

> ### ⚠ Two caveats that bound every number here
>
> **1. Same spec, NOT same instance (environment class differs).** The two connectors run against *dedicated* targets: **Spark = `production`**, **Kafka Connect = `staging`**. Each connector's own H/P *ratio* gate (its Tab 1) is unaffected — both arms share one target. But the **absolute** cross-connector numbers on this tab (rows/s, server CPU per Mrows) **straddle a production/staging boundary**. Every chart shows `environment_class` beside `clickhouse_version` so you can see it. Read absolute gaps as *directional*, not as a certified same-hardware benchmark.
>
> **2. Matched-dataset only (comparisons require equal volume).** A cross-connector comparison is valid **only** when both connectors ran the **same `(dataset, rows_expected)`** — see contract §5/§6. Today **Kafka runs `hits`@10M and Spark runs `hits`@~100M**, so there is **no matched bucket** and the comparison charts + efficiency table below are **honestly empty**. That is expected: a Kafka **100M graduation** is a deliberate future instrument event; when it lands, the matched side populates automatically (the rule self-maintains — no dashboard edit). Until then, the **UNMATCHED CONTEXT** table at the bottom shows each connector's numbers side-by-side, *clearly labelled non-comparable*.
charts.description: PERMANENT CAVEAT BANNER (markdown) for Tab 5. States the two bounds on every number: (1) environment_class differs (Spark=production, Kafka=staging), so absolute cross-connector numbers straddle a prod/staging boundary — per-connector H/P gates are unaffected (contract §6); (2) comparisons are matched-dataset only (equal dataset+rows_expected), and today the matched side is honestly empty (Kafka 10M vs Spark 100M) pending a Kafka 100M graduation. This is markdown by design so the reader is told WHY the comparison charts are empty rather than seeing broken charts.

charts.series_note: TWO series keyed by connector_label (Spark, Kafka Connect), both plotting the ALIASED rows_per_sec (§2.2: Spark throughput_rows_per_sec + Kafka drain_rows_per_sec folded to one series in v_xconn) vs run_started_at. matched_dataset=1 so only comparable (equal dataset+rows_expected) runs appear — EMPTY today.
charts.description: VERIFIED THROUGHPUT (rows/s), Spark vs Kafka Connect, on the MATCHED dataset. The §2.2 alias folds each connector's distinct headline (Spark throughput_rows_per_sec, Kafka drain_rows_per_sec) into one rows_per_sec series so the two lines share an axis. HOW TO READ: each point is a head-arm (code-under-test) tier-1 run at a shared (dataset, rows_expected); hover shows environment_class + clickhouse_version for that run (they differ across connectors — see the banner). EMPTY TODAY: no matched bucket exists (Kafka 10M vs Spark 100M), so this chart shows no data until a Kafka 100M graduation — that is correct, not a bug. DIRECTION OF GOODNESS: higher is better; the higher line delivered more verified rows/s on equal volume.

charts.series_note: TWO series keyed by connector_label plotting ch_insert_cpu_seconds_per_Mrows (the PINNED cross-connector server-cost name, contract §2.1 / Appendix — NOT server_cpu_per_Mrows) vs run_started_at, matched_dataset=1.
charts.description: SERVER INSERT CPU per Mrows (s/Mrows), Spark vs Kafka Connect, on the MATCHED dataset. This is the server-side cost headline: query_log OSCPUVirtualTime ÷ (delivered rows / 1e6) — how much target CPU each connector's insert path burns per million rows (contract §2.1). Pinned name is ch_insert_cpu_seconds_per_Mrows (the plan's sketch name server_cpu_per_Mrows is NOT used). HOW TO READ: same server cost = the connectors' client-side differences are NOT leaking into server cost; a gap points at an interaction (partitioning, part creation, dedup) one connector triggers more. EMPTY TODAY: no matched bucket (see banner). DIRECTION OF GOODNESS: LOWER is better; the lower line is cheaper on the server per row.

charts.series_note: TWO series keyed by connector_label plotting parts_per_insert (§7 fold: ch_parts_per_insert legacy spelling folded to the pinned name in v_xconn) vs run_started_at, matched_dataset=1.
charts.description: PARTS PER INSERT, Spark vs Kafka Connect, on the MATCHED dataset. Parts created per insert on the target (contract §2.1 tripwire family) — a proxy for how much merge work each connector's write pattern forces on ClickHouse. The legacy ch_parts_per_insert spelling is folded to parts_per_insert (§7). HOW TO READ: ~1.0 means one part per insert (ideal batching); higher means the connector is fragmenting writes and pushing merge load onto the server. EMPTY TODAY: no matched bucket (see banner). DIRECTION OF GOODNESS: LOWER (closer to 1.0) is better; a connector sitting well above 1.0 is generating avoidable server merge work.

charts.series_note: TWO series keyed by connector_label plotting merge_amplification (WATCH-ONLY covariate, contract §2/§3 — no verdict) vs run_started_at, matched_dataset=1.
charts.description: MERGE AMPLIFICATION, Spark vs Kafka Connect, on the MATCHED dataset. Bytes merged ÷ bytes inserted — how much extra I/O the server does merging each connector's parts down (contract §2, WATCH-ONLY: no verdict, it is a covariate that explains a server-cost gap, not a gate). HOW TO READ: pairs with the parts_per_insert chart — high parts_per_insert usually drives high merge_amplification, and together they explain a server-CPU gap on the top-right chart. EMPTY TODAY: no matched bucket (see banner). DIRECTION OF GOODNESS: LOWER is better; less merge amplification = less wasted server I/O per delivered row.

charts.query: SELECT metric_name, unit, spark_median, kafka_median, round(ratio_spark_over_kafka, 4) AS ratio_spark_over_kafka, spark_environment_class, kafka_environment_class, spark_clickhouse_version, kafka_clickhouse_version, spark_n, kafka_n FROM v_xconn_efficiency ORDER BY metric_name
charts.description: EFFICIENCY TABLE — latest-30d MEDIAN per metric, Spark vs Kafka Connect, with the ratio (plan §8). One row per metric_name; ratio_spark_over_kafka = spark_median ÷ kafka_median (always Spark-over-Kafka; the direction of goodness is per-metric, below). Built ON v_xconn and MATCHED-ONLY by construction, so it is HONESTLY EMPTY today (no shared (dataset, rows_expected) bucket — Kafka 10M vs Spark 100M). HOW TO READ each metric's ratio: rows_per_sec is higher_better, so ratio>1 = Spark delivered more rows/s; ch_insert_cpu_seconds_per_Mrows / parts_per_insert / merge_amplification are lower_better (server cost), so ratio<1 = Spark is cheaper on the server, ratio>1 = Kafka is cheaper. WHICH CONNECTOR COSTS THE SERVER MORE, AND IS THE GAP CLIENT OR INTERACTION: read the rows/s ratio against the cost ratios — if one connector is faster (rows/s) AND cheaper (cpu/parts/merge), the gap is a clean client win; if it is faster but MORE expensive on the server, the throughput came at a server-interaction cost. COVARIATES: spark/kafka_environment_class + clickhouse_version columns show the prod/staging + version difference the banner warns about. spark_n/kafka_n are the sample counts behind each median — at n<20 the median is provisional/noisy (task-35 constraint 9 style). DIRECTION OF GOODNESS: per-metric as above; there is no single 'good' ratio direction because the table mixes higher_better and lower_better metrics.

charts.query: SELECT connector_label, metric_name, dataset, rows_expected, value, unit, environment_class, clickhouse_version, if(flagged, flag_reason, '') AS flag_reason, run_started_at FROM v_xconn WHERE matched_dataset=0 AND metric_name IN ('rows_per_sec','ch_insert_cpu_seconds_per_Mrows','parts_per_insert','merge_amplification') ORDER BY metric_name, connector_label, run_started_at DESC
charts.description: UNMATCHED CONTEXT (NON-COMPARABLE) — each connector's headline metrics side-by-side, on their OWN dataset volumes, for the runs that do NOT share a (dataset, rows_expected) bucket. This is where TODAY's data lives (Kafka 10M, Spark 100M). ⚠ THESE ARE NOT A COMPARISON: the two connectors ran DIFFERENT volumes, so the rows/s and cost numbers are not apples-to-apples — the rows_expected column shows the volume mismatch that makes them non-comparable (contract §6: a mismatched row MUST NEVER render as a comparable efficiency number, so it appears only here, clearly separated, never on the shared series above). HOW TO READ: use this to see each connector is alive and roughly where it sits; do NOT compute a cross-connector ratio from it. It also carries environment_class + clickhouse_version + any flag_reason so a marked run is visible. DIRECTION OF GOODNESS: not applicable — this table is context, not a verdict; comparison lives on the matched charts above once a shared bucket exists.
